function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Orbital AI compute satellites would require inputs from multiple segments. What accounting-policy question would arise once manufacturing begins, and which disclosed policies appear most relevant?
I'll start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing to understand the segment structure, intersegment arrangements, and relevant accounting policies.
steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about the accounting-policy questions that would arise when SpaceX begins manufacturing orbital AI compute satellites, which would require inputs from multiple segments (presumably SpaceX's existing segments like Launch Services, Starlink, etc. and potentially xAI). They want to know which disclosed policies from the S-1 filing would be most relevant.

Let me first parse the SpaceX S-1 filing to understand the segment structure and accounting policies disclosed.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spaceX_s1

steps.message: Now let me retrieve several relevant sections from the filing in parallel:
steps.reasoning_content: Good, the filing has been parsed. Now let me retrieve information about the segment structure and accounting policies.

arguments.prompt: What are the business segments described in {{spaceX_s1}}? Include segment names, descriptions, and any discussion of how segments interact or share resources.
What is the revenue recognition policy described in {{spaceX_s1}}? Include details about intersegment revenue, performance obligations, and how revenue is allocated across segments.
What intersegment transactions, intersegment revenue, or transfer pricing policies are disclosed in {{spaceX_s1}}? Include any tables showing intersegment eliminations or allocations.

What accounting policies related to inventory, cost capitalization, R&D costs, and internal-use software or asset development are disclosed in {{spaceX_s1}}? Include policies on when costs are expensed vs capitalized.

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here are the business segments, their descriptions, and how they interact:

### **The Three Operating Segments**
Effective in Q1 2026, the company's Chief Operating Decision Maker (CODM) organizes performance and resource allocation into three segments:

1.  **Space Segment**
    *   **Core Function:** Designs, manufactures, and launches reusable rockets (Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, and the next-generation Starship) to provide high-cadence, reliable, and affordable access to space.
    *   **Revenue Sources:** Launch services for commercial, civil, international, and government customers (e.g., NASA crew/cargo missions, National Security Space Launch missions).

2.  **Connectivity Segment**
    *   **Core Function:** Operates the Starlink worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband network using thousands of satellites in Low-Earth Orbit (LEO).
    *   **Revenue Sources:**
        *   **Starlink Consumer Broadband:** Subscription-based internet for residential users.
        *   **Enterprise & Government Solutions:** Connectivity services for businesses and governments.
        *   **Starlink Mobile:** Satellite-to-mobile texting and voice services.
        *   **Hardware Sales:** Sale of Starlink Kits (terminals).

3.  **AI Segment**
    *   **Core Function:** Operates a vertically integrated AI platform.
    *   **Components & Revenue Sources:**
        *   **X:** A real-time information, entertainment, and free-speech platform (generates revenue from advertising and subscriptions).
        *   **Grok:** A frontier Large Language Model (LLM) powering AI solutions for consumers (via X) and enterprises (via Grok Business and Grok Enterprise, sold on license, consumption, or outcome-based models).
        *   **AI Computational Infrastructure:** The data centers and computing power that support these services.

### **Interaction and Resource Sharing Between Segments**
The segments are deeply interconnected in a **self-reinforcing value creation cycle**:

*   **Space enables Connectivity:** The Space segment's reusable launch capabilities are the **backbone** that allows the rapid deployment and expansion of the Starlink satellite constellation for the Connectivity segment.
*   **Connectivity funds growth:** The Connectivity segment generates predictable, recurring subscription revenue. Profitability from this segment (Segment Adjusted EBITDA positive since 2023) has been used, along with equity capital, to fund investments in both Space and other ventures.
*   **Shared Technological Foundation:** The company leverages a "common technological foundation" across all domains. For example, vertical integration in manufacturing and software development benefits all segments.
*   **AI leverages platform and data:** The AI segment utilizes the X platform for user access and data, and the AI computational infrastructure likely supports operations across the company.
*   **Resource Allocation by CODM:** The CODM assesses performance and allocates resources (capital, talent) to these segments based on each one's **income (loss) from operations**, comparing actual results to historical performance and forecasts. This directs investment towards the segments with the highest growth or strategic need (e.g., heavy recent capex in AI and Starship development).

**In summary:** The company operates three synergistic segments. **Space** provides the launch infrastructure that scales **Connectivity**, which generates reliable revenue to fund future investments across all segments, including the computationally intensive **AI** business. The CODM manages this portfolio by allocating capital based on segment-level profitability metrics.

Based on the provided excerpts from the financial statement notes, here is a detailed breakdown of SpaceX's revenue recognition policies, with a focus on intersegment revenue, performance obligations, and segment allocation.

### 1. Overview of Revenue Recognition by Segment
SpaceX recognizes revenue across three primary segments: **Space, Connectivity, and AI**. The recognition method (over time or at a point in time) and the treatment of performance obligations differ significantly based on the segment and the nature of the contract.

### 2. Space Segment Revenue Recognition
*   **Revenue Sources:** Launch Services (for deploying customer payloads) and Launch & Development (for developing spacecraft and providing services for government programs).
*   **Performance Obligations & Recognition:**
    *   **Launch Services:** Recognized at a **point in time** (upon launch or deployment) because flight hardware is interchangeable and has minimal unique engineering costs. Costs are deferred until launch.
    *   **Launch & Development:** Recognized **over time** using the **cost-to-cost input method**. This applies when the created asset has no alternative use and there is an enforceable right to payment. Revenue is recorded based on costs incurred to date versus total estimated costs at completion.
*   **Intersegment Revenue & Capacity Allocation:**
    *   Launches dedicated to deploying the company's own **Starlink satellites do not generate intersegment revenue** for the Space segment.
    *   Instead, the costs of these internal launches are **capitalized** as part of "Property, plant, and equipment" within the **Connectivity segment** and depreciated over time.
    *   The company allocates significant launch capacity to its Connectivity segment and expects to do so for its **AI segment in the future**. The Space segment's reported revenue reflects only customer-facing activities.

### 3. Connectivity Segment Revenue Recognition
*   **Revenue Sources:** Broadband and Starlink Mobile services (Consumer & Enterprise), Starshield contracts, and Starlink Kit sales.
*   **Performance Obligations & Recognition:**
    *   **Consumer/Enterprise Services:** Contracts typically contain **multiple performance obligations**—(i) broadband services and (ii) the Starlink Kit. The transaction price is allocated based on standalone selling prices.
        *   *Services:* Recognized **over time** as the customer simultaneously consumes benefits. For fixed-price subscriptions, revenue is recognized ratably; for variable plans, based on data usage. Amounts received in advance are recorded as deferred revenue.
        *   *Starlink Kit:* Recognized at a **point in time** (upon delivery or installation).
    *   **Starshield:** Long-term, fixed-price contracts recognized **over time** using the **cost-to-cost input method**, similar to Launch & Development contracts. Significant management judgment is required for cost estimates.
*   **Deferred Revenue:** Primarily relates to advanced payments for enterprise/government contracts and services.

### 4. AI Segment Revenue Recognition
*   **Revenue Sources:** Advertising, subscriptions (e.g., X, Grok), data licensing, and API access.
*   **Performance Obligations & Recognition:**
    *   **Advertising:** Recognized at a **point in time** when the ad is delivered and user engagement meets criteria. The company acts as **principal** (gross revenue) for direct sales via the X platform and as **agent** (net revenue) for sales through SSP partners.
    *   **Subscriptions:** Recognized **ratably over the subscription term**.
    *   **Data Licensing:** May have single or multiple performance obligations. Recognition depends on the arrangement: over time for future IP, point-in-time for historical IP, or ratably for fixed fees. Minimum guarantees are recognized straight-line, with excess royalties recognized over the term.
    *   **API Services:** Stand-ready obligation recognized either **ratably** (for subscription access) or **as consumed** (for usage-based/token processing).

### 5. Key Judgments and Disclosures
*   **Cost-to-Cost Method:** Used for long-term contracts in the Space (Launch & Development) and Connectivity (Starshield) segments. Requires significant estimates of total costs, including materials, labor, and technological changes. Changes in estimates are recognized on a cumulative basis.
*   **Backlog:** As of December 31, 2025, total backlog was **$28.4 billion**, of which $12.1 billion was deferred revenue. Approximately 32% is expected to be recognized within one year.
*   **Segment Allocation:** Revenue is clearly segregated. For example:
    *   **Products revenue** (mainly Starlink Kits) is entirely attributable to the **Connectivity** segment.
    *   **Enterprise & Government revenue** within Connectivity includes Starlink Mobile services.
    *   **Space segment revenue** excludes all internal constellation launches.

In summary, SpaceX's revenue recognition is tailored to each segment's business model, with strict rules preventing intersegment profit recognition for internal launches while carefully allocating capacity across segments. The policies involve significant judgment, particularly for long-term, complex contracts using the cost-to-cost method.

Based on the provided excerpts from SpaceX's financial disclosures, here is a breakdown of the disclosed intersegment transactions, revenue, and transfer pricing policies:

### 1. **Intersegment Transactions & Revenue Recognition**
   - **Starlink Launches:** For launches of Starlink satellites (which are part of the **Connectivity segment**), the **Space segment does not recognize any intersegment revenue**. Instead, the launch costs are **capitalized** as part of the cost of the Starlink satellites, recorded in *Property, plant, and equipment, net* on the consolidated balance sheet.
   - **Capacity Allocation:** The Space segment allocates a "significant amount" of launch capacity to the Connectivity segment. It also expects to allocate a "significant amount" of capacity to the **AI segment** in the future.
   - **External Revenue Only:** The Space segment's reported revenue **only reflects customer launches and customer activities**. Intersegment launches (like those for Starlink) are not included in segment revenue.

### 2. **Transfer Pricing / Accounting Policy**
   - The transfer pricing for intersegment launches is based on **cost capitalization**, not on recognizing intersegment revenue or profit. This means the Space segment incurs launch costs, which are then transferred to the Connectivity segment by capitalizing them into the asset (satellites) that the Connectivity segment owns and operates.
   - This approach aligns with the **cost-to-cost input method** used for Launch and Development revenue recognition, where costs are tracked and allocated to specific projects or assets.

### 3. **Financial Tables & Intersegment Eliminations**
   - The provided financial tables (e.g., for Q1 2026 and FY 2025) present revenue and expenses for each reportable segment (Space, Connectivity, AI) and then sum to a **"Total Reportable Segments"** column.
   - There are **no explicit columns or line items labeled "Intersegment Eliminations"** in these excerpts. However, the **"Total Reportable Segments"** figures likely reflect the **net effect after intersegment eliminations** (e.g., the removal of any intersegment revenue or cost allocations). For example:
     - The Space segment's revenue ($619M in Q1 2026) excludes intersegment launch revenue.
     - The Connectivity segment's cost of revenue ($1,651M in Q1 2026) likely includes the capitalized launch costs for Starlink satellites (transferred from the Space segment).
   - The consolidated financial statements (which combine all segments) would show the **eliminated intersegment balances** in the consolidation process, but these eliminations are not broken out in the segment-level tables provided.

### Summary of Key Points:
- **Intersegment Transactions:** Primarily consist of **launch services for Starlink satellites** (Connectivity segment) and potentially for future AI segment needs.
- **Revenue Recognition:** **No intersegment revenue** is recognized for these launches; costs are **capitalized** into the assets of the receiving segment.
- **Transfer Pricing Policy:** Based on **cost capitalization**, not market-based pricing or revenue recognition. This ensures that intersegment profits are not recognized until the underlying assets (e.g., satellites) generate external revenue.
- **Financial Disclosure:** Segment tables show **net segment performance after intersegment eliminations**, with the "Total Reportable Segments" column reflecting the combined results of all segments without intersegment profit or loss.

Based on the provided excerpts from the Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements, here is a summary of the key accounting policies related to inventory, cost capitalization, R&D costs, and internal-use software/asset development.

### 1. Inventory
*   **Classification:** Raw materials and work-in-progress for production (launch vehicles, Starlink Kits), and finished goods (Starlink Kits, Falcon 9/Falcon Heavy second stages).
*   **Valuation Method:** Computed using standard cost or weighted average cost, approximating actual cost on a first-in, first-out (FIFO) basis.
*   **Reporting Value:** Stated at the **lower of cost or net realizable value**.
*   **Write-downs:** Recorded in **Cost of revenue** for estimated obsolescence or unmarketable inventories based on assumptions about future demand and technological changes.

### 2. Research and Development (R&D) Costs
*   **Expensing Policy:** **Expensed as incurred.**
*   **Included Costs:** Materials, employee compensation (salaries, benefits, share-based compensation), contractor costs, cloud computing, data services, equipment leases, depreciation for R&D equipment, allocated overhead, and certain engineering service costs where intellectual property is retained.

### 3. Software Development & Asset Capitalization
*   **Current Policy for Software Marketed to Customers:** Software development costs are **expensed as incurred**. Costs incurred prior to establishing technological feasibility are part of R&D expenses.
*   **Future Change (ASU 2025-06):** An upcoming standard will simplify capitalization guidance for **internal-use software** by removing prescriptive development stages. The company is evaluating the impact.
*   **General Capitalization Policy for Property, Plant, and Equipment:**
    *   **Costs Expensed:** Routine maintenance and repairs that **do not extend** an asset's useful life.
    *   **Costs Capitalized:** Significant refurbishment, renewals, and enhancements that **increase functionality, output, or expected useful life**. These costs are then depreciated over the asset's remaining useful life.
    *   **Satellite Costs:** Include building costs (parts, labor, overhead) and **capitalized launch costs** incurred to put the satellite into orbit.
    *   **Interest Capitalization:** Certain interest costs are capitalized during the construction of significant long-term assets once activities to prepare the asset begin, using the weighted average borrowing rate.

### 4. Specific Asset Classification & Depreciation (Launch Vehicles)
*   **Reusable Components (Boosters, Fairings, Engines):** Classified as **Property, Plant, and Equipment, net** and depreciated based on the expected number of average flights (not a simple straight-line time basis).
*   **Expendable Components (Falcon Second Stages):** Recorded as **Inventory** until launched or assigned, then charged to **Cost of revenue**.
*   **Depreciation for Satellites:** Based on estimated useful life (e.g., 5 years for broadband satellites), considering on-orbit performance, propellant life, and technology transitions.

### Summary: When Costs are Expensed vs. Capitalized
The company's policies create a clear framework for cost treatment:
*   **Expensed Immediately (as part of operating expenses):**
    *   All **Research and Development** costs.
    *   **Routine maintenance and repairs**.
    *   **Inventory write-downs**.
    *   **Software development costs** (currently, for marketed products).
*   **Capitalized (recorded as an asset and depreciated/amortized over time):**
    *   Costs of **long-lived assets** (property, equipment, satellites) including purchase price, certain direct costs, and launch costs.
    *   **Major refurbishments or enhancements** that extend an asset's life or improve its functionality.
    *   **Interest costs** during the construction period of qualifying assets.
    *   (Future change for internal-use software under ASU 2025-06).

**Note:** The specific treatment for Starship is highlighted as a development-stage asset where most costs are currently expensed as R&D, with an expectation to capitalize costs once the vehicle is commercialized.

steps.message: Let me retrieve additional details on the specific policies that would be most relevant to a multi-segment product like orbital AI compute satellites:
steps.reasoning_content: Now let me get more specific information about how the company handles cross-segment assets and any discussion about future products or services that might span multiple segments.

arguments.prompt: In {{spaceX_s1}}, what does the filing say about how satellite costs are capitalized, including the components of satellite cost (building, launch, etc.), the useful life assumptions, and the depreciation methodology for satellites? Also describe how Starship development costs are treated.

In {{spaceX_s1}}, describe the segment reporting methodology. How does the CODM evaluate segment performance? What metric is used (income from operations, adjusted EBITDA, etc.)? How are shared costs allocated across segments? Is there any discussion of how costs for products serving multiple segments would be allocated?

In {{spaceX_s1}}, what does the filing disclose about the relationship between the AI segment and the Connectivity segment or Space segment regarding satellite launches, orbital infrastructure, or compute? Is there any discussion of Starshield, orbital compute, or AI satellites?

results.content:
Based on the provided financial filing excerpts, here is a detailed breakdown of the accounting treatment for satellite costs and Starship development costs.

### 1. Satellite Cost Capitalization

**Components of Capitalized Satellite Cost:**
The cost of a satellite asset includes all costs necessary to prepare it for its intended use. These components are:
*   **Build Costs:** Costs to construct the satellite, which include raw materials, parts, direct labor, and allocated manufacturing overhead.
*   **Capitalized Launch Costs:** A significant portion of the launch costs incurred by the Space segment to deliver the satellite to orbit are capitalized as part of the satellite's historical cost. This includes an allocation of the flight vehicle (booster and second stage) costs used in the launch.

**Useful Life Assumptions:**
The company estimates the useful life of its satellite assets based on engineering studies and operational factors.
*   **Broadband Satellites (Starlink):** Estimated useful life of **5 years**.
*   **First-Generation Mobile Satellites:** Estimated useful life of **3 years**.

**Depreciation Methodology:**
*   **Method:** Satellites are depreciated using the **straight-line method** over their estimated useful lives.
*   **Adjustments:** If operational experience (e.g., on-orbit performance, propellant life, technology evolution) indicates the useful life is shorter or longer than originally estimated, the depreciation rate is adjusted prospectively to reflect the revised estimate.

### 2. Treatment of Starship Development Costs

**Current Treatment (During Development Phase):**
*   Starship is currently in the **development stage**.
*   As such, the majority of its associated costs are **expensed as incurred** under **Research and Development (R&D)**.
*   These R&D costs include test flight hardware, Raptor engines, employee compensation, tooling, equipment depreciation, and allocated overhead.

**Future Treatment (Upon Commercialization):**
*   The company states that once Starship is commercialized (i.e., begins delivering customer payload to orbit), its costs will generally be **capitalized**.
*   These capitalized costs will then be **depreciated to Cost of Revenue** over the asset's useful life, with the expense recognized in the operating segment that utilizes the vehicle (e.g., the Space segment for launch services).

### Summary of Key Accounting Policies:
*   **Satellites:** Capitalized costs include build and launch expenses, depreciated straight-line over a 3-5 year useful life based on operational estimates.
*   **Starship:** Currently R&D (expensed as incurred); will shift to capitalization and depreciation upon reaching commercial service.

Based on the provided excerpts from the Segment Reporting note and MD&A, here is a description of the segment reporting methodology:

### Segment Structure & Realignment
Following mergers, the company realigned its reportable segments effective in Q1 2026. The Chief Executive Officer, as the Chief Operating Decision Maker (CODM), now organizes the company, manages resource allocations, and measures performance across three segments:
1.  **Space** (includes launch services, Starship development)
2.  **Connectivity** (primarily Starlink)
3.  **AI** (following the acquisition of xAI)

Historical segment presentations have been recast to conform to this new structure.

### CODM Performance Evaluation & Key Metric
The CODM evaluates segment performance and allocates resources based on a specific metric:

> **"The Company’s CODM assesses performance and allocates resources to operating segments based on segment income (loss) from operations by comparing actual income (loss) from operations to historical results and previously forecasted financial information."**

This indicates that **segment income (loss) from operations** is the primary GAAP measure used for internal performance evaluation and planning.

Additionally, management uses a non-GAAP supplemental measure for analysis:

> **"Segment Adjusted EBITDA is defined as segment income (loss) from operations excluding (i) depreciation and amortization, (ii) share-based compensation, (iii) restructuring charges, and (iv) impairment. Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA are key performance measures that our management uses to assess our financial performance as well as for internal planning and forecasting purposes."**

Both metrics are presented in the segment data tables.

### Allocation of Costs and Shared Expenses
The provided excerpts **do not contain a detailed discussion on the methodology for allocating shared costs or corporate expenses to the segments.** The segment financial tables (e.g., in the Notes to the Financial Statements) present line items such as "Cost of revenue," "Research and development," and "Selling, general, and administrative" directly for each segment, implying that these costs are directly attributable or have been allocated to the segments.

However, the text does note limitations of the non-GAAP measures and states that Adjusted EBITDA (the consolidated measure) is reconciled from "Net income (loss)," which includes items like consolidated interest expense and taxes that are not allocated to segments. Therefore, it is clear that certain **income and expense items at the consolidated level are not allocated to segments** and are evaluated only at the company level.

**In summary:** The CODM uses **segment income (loss) from operations** as the primary GAAP measure for performance evaluation and resource allocation. The company also tracks **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** as a key supplemental non-GAAP metric. While segment-level expense detail is provided, the specific policies for allocating shared or corporate costs to segments are not described in the given excerpts.

Based on the provided financial filing excerpts, SpaceX explicitly details a deep, integrated relationship between its **AI segment** and its **Space/Connectivity segments**, centered on launch capabilities, orbital infrastructure, and compute. Here is a breakdown of the disclosed relationships:

### 1. **Foundation: Space Segment Provides Launch Capability**
The Space segment is the foundational enabler for both Connectivity and AI segments. The filing states that the high cadence and reusability of its launch vehicles underpin the performance of all segments.
*   **For AI:** The company allocates "a significant amount of launch capacity to our Connectivity segment, and expect[s] to allocate a significant amount to our AI segment in the future." The goal is to deploy a "large fleet of orbital AI compute."

### 2. **Connectivity Segment Provides the Orbital Network Template and Infrastructure**
The Starlink network is the proven blueprint and backbone for the future orbital AI compute network.
*   **Evolution of Technology:** SpaceX states it has "already solved many of the significant technical hurdles to evolving connectivity satellites into AI compute satellites" through its expertise in mass production, deployment, and network operations.
*   **Global Data Transport:** The existing Starlink constellation, with its 23,000+ inter-satellite lasers, will provide the "global network [that] allows data from our AI compute satellites to reach ground stations," enabling low-latency connectivity for orbital AI systems.
*   **Shared Operational Expertise:** The company will leverage its experience operating ~9,600 Starlink satellites (including automated collision avoidance) to manage the orbital AI compute fleet.

### 3. **The AI Segment's Orbital Compute Strategy (AI Satellites)**
The AI segment is developing **orbital AI compute infrastructure** as a core part of its strategy.
*   **Timeline and Scale:** Deployment of "orbital AI compute satellites" is expected "as early as 2028." These are described as "next-generation, high-performance AI compute satellites built for high volume."
*   **Integrated Advantage:** The strategy hinges on vertical integration: leveraging aerospace manufacturing and launch capabilities (Space), satellite design and network operations (Connectivity), and AI model development (AI) to build a "massive, coherent constellation of compute" in orbit.
*   **Terrestrial Support:** The AI segment is also building gigawatt-scale terrestrial data centers (e.g., Colossus II), which the filing suggests will inform and support the design and testing of modular, satellite-form-factor compute hardware for orbit.

### 4. **Starshield: The Government-Facing Connectivity Product**
**Starshield** is explicitly mentioned as a component of the **Connectivity segment**, not the AI segment.
*   It is described as "a secure satellite network designed specifically for government customers and national security applications."
*   Revenue for Starshield is generated via "long-term contracts" within the Connectivity segment.

### Summary of Relationships
The filing portrays the three segments as a synergistic "vertically-integrated innovation engine":
*   **Space Segment:** Provides the launch vehicle "highway" to orbit for all satellites (Starlink, future AI compute, and Starshield).
*   **Connectivity Segment:** Operates the orbital network (Starlink/Starshield) that will also serve as the data transport layer for orbital AI compute, and provides the core satellite technology.
*   **AI Segment:** Develops the models (Grok) and solutions, and is building the compute infrastructure—both on Earth and, crucially, **in orbit**—to host AI workloads at a scale that leverages the unique advantages of space (e.g., power, cooling).

In essence, the AI segment's most ambitious infrastructure plans are directly dependent on and enabled by the launch, manufacturing, and operational capabilities established by the Space and Connectivity segments.

steps.message: Now let me check for any additional details on the specific accounting treatment that might be planned for orbital compute satellites, and the intersegment cost capitalization methodology:
steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me also look for any discussion of how the company plans to account for the orbital AI compute satellites specifically, or any discussion of cost allocation methodology for multi-segment assets.

arguments.prompt: In {{spaceX_s1}}, is there any specific discussion of how orbital AI compute satellite costs will be accounted for? Which segment will own these assets? How will costs from the Space segment (launch) and Connectivity segment (satellite design/manufacturing) be allocated to the AI segment? Are there any risk factors or forward-looking statements about accounting for these multi-segment assets?

In {{spaceX_s1}}, what risk factors are disclosed related to accounting complexity, segment reporting, intersegment transactions, or the challenges of operating across multiple business segments? Include any risks related to cost allocation, transfer pricing, or revenue recognition across segments.

results.content:
Based on the provided prospectus excerpts, here is a synthesis of the key points regarding the accounting and allocation for orbital AI compute satellite costs:

### 1. **Owning Segment and Asset Capitalization**
*   **Primary Owner:** The **AI segment** is identified as the segment that will operate the orbital AI compute infrastructure. The financial statements show significant **capital expenditures for the AI segment** (e.g., $7,723 million for Q1 2026, $3,201 million for 2025), which are expected to fund compute infrastructure.
*   **Accounting Treatment:** The costs for designing, manufacturing, and deploying the AI compute satellites would be **capitalized as "Property, Plant, and Equipment"** (or potentially a new category within it) on the AI segment's balance sheet. The note on Property, Plant, and Equipment indicates that **construction-in-progress** includes "AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service," confirming this capitalization practice.

### 2. **Cost Allocation Between Segments**
The company operates a **vertically integrated model**, and inter-segment transactions are handled as follows:
*   **Launch Services (Space Segment to AI Segment):** Costs incurred by the **Space segment** to launch AI compute satellites are **not recognized as revenue** for the Space segment. Instead, these launch costs are **capitalized into the asset value of the satellites** (Property, Plant, and Equipment) held by the **AI segment**. This is directly stated in the revenue recognition policy: *"For launches of our Starlink satellites [and by extension, AI satellites], the Company does not recognize any inter-segment revenue, rather those launch costs are capitalized in satellites in Property, plant, and equipment, net."*
*   **Satellite Manufacturing & Technology:** While not explicitly detailed, the document implies that the **Connectivity segment's** expertise in satellite design and manufacturing would be leveraged. Costs from this segment for AI satellite production would likely be transferred at cost to the AI segment and capitalized as part of the satellite's total cost. The Connectivity segment's role is highlighted in providing the foundational "modular, satellite form factors" and the laser mesh network.

### 3. **Forward-Looking Statements & Risk Factors**
The prospectus contains multiple cautions about this strategy:
*   **Technical & Capital Risk:** The development of orbital AI compute is described as involving "significant technical complexity, unproven technologies," and requiring "substantial and ongoing investments" over extended time horizons. The timeline and launch cadence "may be difficult or impossible to determine."
*   **Financial Impact:** The company explicitly warns that it "may choose to increase or accelerate the pace of any of these investments... which could result in periods of **reduced profitability or increased losses** as we prioritize long-term growth over near-term financial performance." The AI segment is expected to have a "multi-year investment horizon before these deployments translate into sustained positive AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA."
*   **Deployment Timeline:** The target for beginning to deploy orbital AI compute satellites is **as early as 2028**, subject to the successful resolution of the technical and capital challenges mentioned.

### Summary of Key Accounting & Allocation Principles:
1.  **Capitalization:** All direct costs (satellite manufacturing, launch, integration) for orbital AI compute infrastructure will be capitalized as assets.
2.  **Segment Ownership:** The capitalized assets will reside on the **AI segment's** balance sheet.
3.  **Inter-Segment Transfers:** Costs from the **Space segment** (launch) and **Connectivity segment** (satellite tech/manufacturing) will be allocated to the **AI segment at cost**, without generating inter-segment profit for the transferring segment. This is a consistent policy applied to Starlink and extended to AI.
4.  **Disclosure:** The **segment reporting tables** will continue to reflect each segment's own revenue and expenses. The AI segment's financials will show the operational results (revenue, loss from operations, CapEx) for its AI compute activities, including the depreciation of these orbital assets once operational.

In essence, SpaceX plans to use its integrated model to **internalize the massive capital cost** of building an orbital AI fleet, allocating costs across its segments without inter-segment markups, while clearly warning investors of the substantial financial risk and lack of near-term profitability associated with this venture.

Based on a review of the provided text, here are the disclosed risk factors related to accounting complexity, segment reporting, intersegment transactions, and the challenges of operating across multiple business segments:

### **1. Risks from Accounting Complexity and Judgment**
*   **Revenue Recognition Estimates for Long-Term Contracts:** The company uses the "cost-to-cost input method" for long-term, over-time contracts in the Starshield business. This requires significant management judgment to estimate total costs at completion and the measurement of progress toward completion. Developing these estimates involves complex assumptions regarding labor hours, material costs, shared cost allocations, and technological changes. Errors or changes in these estimates can materially impact revenue and profit recognition.
*   **Complex Multi-Performance Obligation Contracts:** Contracts, particularly in the Connectivity segment for Starlink, often contain multiple distinct performance obligations (e.g., hardware kit and ongoing service). Allocating the transaction price to each obligation based on standalone selling prices requires judgment and complexity.
*   **Estimates in Cost of Revenue:** The cost of revenue includes significant judgments related to inventory obsolescence, warranty obligations, and infrastructure cost allocations. While current warranty expenses are noted as immaterial, such estimates are inherently subject to judgment and change.

### **2. Risks from Segment Reporting and Interdependencies**
*   **Structural Interdependencies and Cost Allocation:** The business model is vertically integrated, creating inherent interdependencies. The **Space segment** provides the launch services and satellite constellation that form the infrastructure for the **Connectivity segment**. Furthermore, the company acknowledges that infrastructure costs (for facilities and data centers) and other shared costs are **allocated overhead** across segments. This creates complexity and potential risks in accurately measuring segment profitability.
*   **Transfer Pricing Risk:** The text references "significant transactions with related parties." While details are in Note 18, this inherently implies the existence of intersegment transfers of goods or services (e.g., launches for Connectivity's satellites). The accounting for these transfers and the determination of their pricing are complex and subject to scrutiny.
*   **Challenges in Evaluating Segment Performance:** The financial statements present segment-level data, including non-GAAP measures like **Segment Adjusted EBITDA**. Investors must understand that these metrics exclude certain costs (like share-based compensation and depreciation) and rely on internal allocations. The distinct economics and maturity levels of the segments (with the AI segment described as "recently formed" and "early stage") make consolidated and segment performance analysis challenging.

### **3. Risks from Operating Across Diverse Business Segments**
*   **Integration and Execution Risks:** The **AI segment** is specifically called out as being "recently formed, still being integrated," and operating in a rapidly evolving industry. This presents significant integration, execution, and competitive risks as the company attempts to manage and grow businesses with vastly different operating models, cost structures, and capital needs.
*   **Diverse Regulatory Environments:** Each segment faces distinct and complex regulatory landscapes:
    *   **Space:** International space laws, launch licensing, and spectrum allocation.
    *   **Connectivity:** Telecommunications regulations in over 150 markets (e.g., FCC in the U.S.), requiring licenses and spectrum access.
    *   **AI:** Evolving regulatory and ethical standards for artificial intelligence.
    Managing compliance across these diverse regimes is a complex operational challenge.
*   **Conflicting Capital Allocation Demands:** All segments are capital-intensive, but for different reasons: rocket/satellite R&D and manufacturing (Space/Connectivity), global subscriber infrastructure (Connectivity), and massive GPU and data center build-outs (AI). The company notes that it has a history of managing investment cycles, but prioritizing and funding these concurrent, large-scale demands across segments is a key risk to financial condition.
*   **Concentration of Key Infrastructure:** The company's operations across all segments are critically dependent on a limited set of assets (satellite constellation, launch vehicles, ground stations, data centers). A failure or interruption in any part of this infrastructure, due to accident, cyberattack, or technical issue, could have cascading adverse effects across all business lines.

In summary, the disclosures highlight that the company's integrated, multi-segment structure creates significant accounting complexity (especially in revenue and cost recognition), poses challenges for transparent financial reporting and analysis, and exposes the business to execution, regulatory, and operational risks that require sophisticated management to mitigate.

steps.message: I now have a comprehensive understanding of the disclosed segment structure, accounting policies, and the orbital AI compute strategy. Let me synthesize this into a complete answer.
steps.reasoning_content:
I now have a comprehensive picture. Let me synthesize this into a thorough answer about the accounting-policy questions that would arise once manufacturing of orbital AI compute satellites begins, and which disclosed policies appear most relevant.

The key issues are:

1. **Intersegment cost allocation / transfer pricing**: Orbital AI compute satellites would require inputs from at least three segments:
   - Space segment: launch services
   - Connectivity segment: satellite design, manufacturing know-how, network infrastructure (laser mesh)
   - AI segment: the AI compute hardware/software, and the segment that will own/operate the satellites

2. **Cost capitalization vs. 

R&D expense**: The fundamental question is whether costs should be immediately expensed as R&D or capitalized as assets and depreciated over time. Starship currently expenses everything as R&D, but satellites are capitalized once they have established useful lives.

3. **Useful life determination**: AI compute satellites represent a novel asset class without established precedent, so the company needs to determine appropriate useful life assumptions.

4. **Intersegment profit elimination**: The policy appears to be no intersegment revenue—just capitalizing costs—which raises questions about proper allocation methodologies.

5. **Segment allocation of shared costs**: How to allocate costs that benefit multiple segments.

6. **Revenue recognition**: If these satellites generate external revenue, I need to understand what performance obligations apply. Now I'm thinking through the most directly relevant accounting policies.

arguments.final_result:
## Accounting-Policy Questions Arising from Orbital AI Compute Satellite Manufacturing

### The Core Policy Question: Multi-Segment Intersegment Cost Allocation and Capitalization

Once manufacturing of orbital AI compute satellites begins, **the central accounting-policy question** will be: **How should costs incurred across multiple segments—Space (launch), Connectivity (satellite design/manufacturing/network infrastructure), and AI (compute hardware/software and ultimate asset ownership)—be measured, allocated, and capitalized, and what transfer-pricing methodology should govern the intersegment flows?**

This is not a theoretical exercise. The S-1 explicitly states that the company expects to "allocate a significant amount of launch capacity to our AI segment in the future" and that it has "already solved many of the significant technical hurdles to evolving connectivity satellites into AI compute satellites." The AI segment's orbital compute infrastructure is expected to begin deployment "as early as 2028."

---

### Specific Accounting-Policy Questions That Would Arise

**1. Intersegment Transfer Pricing: Cost-Based vs. Market-Based**

The filing's existing precedent is cost-capitalization without intersegment profit recognition. For Starlink satellites, "the Company does not recognize any inter-segment revenue, rather those launch costs are capitalized in satellites in Property, plant, and equipment, net." The question is whether this same approach cleanly extends to AI compute satellites, which involve three contributing segments rather than the two (Space → Connectivity) that characterize Starlink.

Specific sub-questions include:
- At what cost basis should the **Connectivity segment** transfer satellite bus/platform technology and manufacturing capacity to the AI segment? Should this include a share of Connectivity's historical R&D amortization?
- Should allocated overhead from shared manufacturing facilities be included in the capitalized cost, and if so, using what allocation methodology?
- Does the absence of disclosed detailed cost-allocation methodologies become a disclosure gap once AI compute satellites represent material capital expenditures?

**2. Capitalization Threshold: R&D Expense vs. Capitalized Asset**

The filing draws a sharp line between expensed R&D and capitalized assets:
- **Starship** is currently in development and its costs are expensed as R&D, with capitalization expected "once the vehicle is commercialized."
- **Starlink satellites** are capitalized (build costs + launch costs) and depreciated over 3–5 years.

AI compute satellites sit at the intersection: they combine novel compute hardware (closer to R&D) with proven satellite bus technology (closer to capitalized asset). The key question is: **At what point does the AI compute satellite program transition from R&D expensing to asset capitalization?** The filing does not specify a threshold for this transition beyond the general policy that assets are capitalized when they have an "alternative use" or when the company has an "enforceable right to payment." For a vertically integrated, proprietary orbital compute platform, determining "alternative use" is non-trivial.

**3. Useful Life Estimation for a Novel Asset Class**

The filing discloses useful lives of **5 years for broadband satellites** and **3 years for first-generation mobile satellites**, based on "on-orbit performance, propellant life, and technology transitions." AI compute satellites would raise the question of whether useful life should be driven by:
- Hardware degradation in the space radiation environment
- The rapid obsolescence cycle of AI compute hardware (GPUs/accelerators typically become obsolete in 2–3 years on Earth)
- Orbital mechanics and propellant life
- The pace of technology transitions in AI models

This is a significant judgment call. A 3-year useful life (reflecting compute obsolescence) versus a 5-year life (reflecting physical satellite lifespan) would materially affect depreciation expense in the AI segment.

**4. Segment Reporting Integrity: Who Bears What Cost?**

The CODM evaluates segment performance using **segment income (loss) from operations**. How costs are allocated directly affects the reported profitability of each segment. Questions include:
- Should the **Connectivity segment** bear any cost for developing satellite technology that is repurposed for AI compute, or should all such costs transfer to the AI segment?
- How should shared R&D costs (e.g., inter-satellite laser technology, which serves both Starlink connectivity and AI compute data transport) be allocated?
- Will the segment tables need enhanced disclosure to explain cost allocations once AI compute satellites represent a material use of Space and Connectivity segment resources?

The filing notes that the company's non-GAAP measures exclude certain costs and that "investors should not consider Segment Adjusted EBITDA in isolation." Multi-segment asset construction amplifies the analytical challenge of interpreting segment profitability.

**5. Construction-in-Progress and Depreciation Commencement**

The filing discloses that **construction-in-progress** includes "AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service." For orbital AI compute satellites, the question is when depreciation commences:
- Upon satellite manufacture completion?
- Upon launch to orbit?
- Upon integration into an operational constellation delivering compute services?

Given that Starlink satellite build costs and launch costs are both capitalized before the satellite generates revenue, the likely treatment is capitalization at launch with depreciation upon entering service. But the filing does not explicitly address staged capitalization for multi-component assets built across segments.

**6. Revenue Recognition if Compute Services are Sold Externally**

If the AI segment sells orbital compute capacity to third-party customers, a new performance obligation framework would be needed. The AI segment currently recognizes API/compute revenue on a consumption or subscription basis. Extending this to orbital compute would raise questions about:
- Whether the satellite constellation constitutes a single deliverable or multiple performance obligations
- Whether customers purchase access (stand-ready obligation) or specific compute time (consumed service)
- How to allocate the transaction price if bundled with terrestrial compute, ground station connectivity, and AI model access

---

### Most Relevant Disclosed Policies

Based on the analysis above, the following disclosed policies appear most directly relevant:

| Policy Area | Disclosed Treatment | Relevance to Orbital AI Compute |
|---|---|---|
| **Intersegment launch costs** | No intersegment revenue; costs capitalized into receiving segment's assets | Directly applicable—Space segment launch costs for AI satellites would be capitalized into AI segment PP&E |
| **Satellite cost capitalization** | Build costs + capitalized launch costs; depreciated straight-line over useful life | Establishes the framework, but AI compute hardware introduces useful-life uncertainty |
| **R&D expensing** | Expensed as incurred until commercialization | Critical question: when does the AI compute satellite program cross from R&D to capitalized asset? |
| **Starship development** | Currently R&D; to be capitalized upon commercialization | Provides the closest precedent for the R&D-to-capitalization transition |
| **Cost-to-cost input method** | Used for long-term contracts (Starshield, Launch & Development) | May apply if orbital compute involves long-term government or enterprise contracts |
| **Inventory valuation** | Standard cost / weighted average, FIFO basis, lower of cost or NRV | Relevant for satellite components held in inventory before integration |
| **Interest capitalization** | Capitalized during construction of qualifying long-lived assets | Would apply to AI compute satellite construction periods |
| **Segment performance metric** | Segment income (loss) from operations; Segment Adjusted EBITDA | Cost allocation methodology directly affects reported segment profitability |
| **Construction-in-progress** | Includes "AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service" | Confirms the capitalization framework for AI infrastructure under construction |

---

### Conclusion

The filing provides a clear intersegment cost-capitalization precedent through the Starlink model (Space → Connectivity), but does not explicitly address the more complex three-segment cost flows that orbital AI compute satellites would require. The primary accounting-policy gap is the **absence of a disclosed methodology for allocating costs when assets are jointly built by the Space and Connectivity segments for ultimate ownership and operation by the AI segment.** Secondary questions around R&D-to-capitalization transition timing and useful-life estimation for compute-intensive orbital hardware represent significant judgment areas that the filing does not resolve. Investors should expect these policies to evolve and potentially require enhanced disclosure as the program moves toward its 2028 deployment target.

**Sources:**
- SpaceX S-1 Filing (May 20, 2026): https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm
